original_abstract:
Teach For America (TFA) has experienced exponential growth since its founding in 1990, ballooning by 2,000% to become a massive recipient of both public and private funding. The program is best known for recruiting college graduates without traditional education backgrounds to serve two-year stints in low-income urban and rural schools. While supporters champion the program as a vital source of "outstanding" talent for struggling districts, critics argue that placing transitory, minimally trained novices in the classrooms of the most vulnerable students is a poor substitute for building a stable, highly skilled professional workforce.

Despite a steady stream of TFA-funded evaluations claiming success, independent peer-reviewed research presents a much more complicated and mixed reality. Studies show that the perceived effectiveness of TFA teachers depends heavily on their specific certification level, their years of experience, and—most importantly—the group of teachers to whom they are being compared. When measured against fully credentialed, experienced professionals, TFA novices often lag behind; however, they may appear more effective when compared to other uncertified or emergency-hire teachers.

Because of high turnover rates—with over 80% of corps members leaving after three years—TFA functions as a costly and temporary "Band-Aid" rather than a sustainable solution to educational inequity. The authors argue that the focus should shift from this model of mixed impact toward evidence-based reforms that offer more substantial and lasting improvements in student achievement. To this end, the paper provides specific recommendations for policymakers and school districts to better navigate the financial and educational trade-offs associated with hiring through TFA.
humanized_abstract:
The teacher-training program Teach For America (TFA) has grown 2,000% since its inception in 1990 and now has been flooded with millions of dollars of public and private money. The program draws primary school teachers from college, often without formal training, to teach in low-income schools for two years. Proponents argue that they bring a “new wave” of talented educators to the neediest schools, but critics contend that teaching for two years with only five weeks of training is not a real or competent way to educate some of the most disadvantaged young minds.

We’ve seen report after report from Teach For America (TFA) about the research that they’ve commissioned, celebrating the work that they do and claiming that the evaluations show that their teachers are having a huge impact. And while it is certainly possible to quibble with the specifics of any study, the reality when the peer-reviewed research on the issue is considered is that the TFA picture is far more complicated. For example, the “effectiveness” of a TFA teacher appears to vary dramatically depending on their level of certification, number of years of teaching experience, and even the pool of other teachers they’re compared to. When their work is compared to that of fully trained, certified and experienced professional teachers — the people that TFA teachers are sent to replace — the results are generally disappointing. The new people, in this context, don’t measure up. But when the comparison is to other uncertified emergency teaching substitutes, then they look much better.

These conclusions come at great cost to our most disadvantaged students. The recent exodus of more than 80% of Teach for America corps members after only three years of teaching may be the most compelling reason why using TFA is a bad use of scarce dollars. Rather than attempt to teach new lessons that they know have been imperfectly learned, we urge policymakers and school districts to rethink their approach to recruitment in light of the trade-offs they face. Rather than continue to seek short-term gains from the inefficient, expensive and imperfect TFA model of recruitment that yields at best a patchwork of modest impacts, they should work to implement more coherent, evidence-based reforms that promise real improvements in the learning of hundreds of thousands of students each year.
